commonMedications.name: Semaglutide / GLP-1 (Ozempic/Wegovy) [P1]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D covers for diabetes (tier 3). $25 copay typical. Without coverage ~$900/mo. IRA negotiated price TBD. New York EPIC program may provide additional assistance.

commonMedications.name: Levothyroxine (Synthroid) [P1]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D generic tier 1; $0-10 copay

commonMedications.name: Atorvastatin 20mg [P1]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D generic tier 1; $0-10 copay

commonMedications.name: Montelukast (Singulair) [P1]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D generic tier 1; $0-10 copay

commonMedications.name: Hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ) [P1]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D generic tier 1; $0-10 copay

commonMedications.name: Olmesartan/Medoxomil (Benicar) [P1]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D generic tier 1-2; $4-15 copay

commonMedications.name: Albuterol inhaler [P1]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D tier 2; $10-25 copay. IRA cap applies.

commonMedications.name: Metformin 500mg [P2]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D generic tier 1; $0-10 copay

commonMedications.name: Allopurinol (gout) [P2]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D generic tier 1; $0-10 copay

commonMedications.name: Lisinopril [P2]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D generic tier 1; $0-10 copay

commonMedications.name: Topiramate (Topamax) [P2]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D generic tier 1-2; $4-15 copay

commonMedications.name: Metoprolol (Lopressor) [P2]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D generic tier 1; $0-10 copay

commonMedications.name: Atorvastatin 20mg [P2]
commonMedications.coverageNote: Medicare Part D generic tier 1; $0-10 copay

medicine.pharmacyAccess: Excellent. Duane Reade, Walgreens, CVS, Rite Aid, and CostPlus pharmacies widely available throughout Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens. Mail-order options through Medicare Part D plans (OptumRx, Express Scripts, Caremark). Many Duane Reade locations offer senior discounts.
medicine.prescriptionSystem: Medicare Part D prescription drug plan. Choose standalone PDP or get coverage through Medicare Advantage. Annual out-of-pocket cap $2,000 under Inflation Reduction Act. New York EPIC program provides additional prescription assistance for eligible seniors.
medicine.insuranceCoverage: Medicare Part D covers most prescriptions with tiered copays. Generic drugs $0-15/month, preferred brands $30-50/month, specialty drugs 25-33% coinsurance up to $2,000 annual cap. New York residents 60+ may qualify for EPIC program.

sources.title: Inflation Reduction Act Drug Provisions - KFF

annualExamCost.notes: Original Medicare does NOT cover routine vision exams. Must have separate vision plan or Medicare Advantage with vision. NYC exam costs 10-15% higher than national average.

vision.insuranceCoverage: Need separate vision plan or Medicare Advantage with vision. Standalone vision plans (VSP, EyeMed) run $18-30/month and cover annual exam + $120-220 frame/lens allowance.
vision.specialistWaitTime: 2-4 weeks for routine eye exams in Manhattan. Shorter waits at retail optometry (LensCrafters, Warby Parker). Ophthalmologist referrals 3-6 weeks. Peak hours Aug-Sept can extend waits.
vision.seniorConsiderations: Medicare covers cataract surgery at NYC's top medical centers (NYU Langone, Columbia, Memorial Sloan Kettering). Glaucoma screening covered for high-risk beneficiaries. Diabetic retinopathy screening covered. One pair of eyeglasses or contacts after cataract surgery.

annualCleaningCost.notes: Original Medicare does NOT cover routine dental. Need separate dental plan or Medicare Advantage with dental. NYC costs 20-25% higher than national average.

dental.insuranceCoverage: Need separate dental plan ($25-60/month) or Medicare Advantage with dental. Standalone plans (Delta Dental, Cigna) typically cover 100% preventive, 50-80% basic, 50% major procedures. Annual maximums $1,000-2,000.
dental.dentistAvailability: Excellent in NYC. Abundant dental practices across all boroughs. Short wait times for routine care (1 week). Specialists (periodontists, prosthodontists) available but may have 2-3 week waits. Many dentists in Upper East Side, Midtown, and Brooklyn serve senior patients.

sources.title: NYC Department of Health Dental Resources

categories.name: Dining Out
categories.seniorDiscounts: Many restaurants offer 10-15% senior discounts. Fine dining and casual establishments throughout NYC. AARP members get discounts at select chains.
categories.notes: Lunch for 2 at moderate NYC restaurant ~$50 + 8.875% tax + 20% tip = ~$62/meal, 2x/week (~8.7/mo) = ~$550/mo. Dinner significantly more expensive. Many neighborhood restaurants in all boroughs.

categories.name: Cable + 1 Gig Internet
categories.seniorDiscounts: Some providers offer senior plans. Verizon Fios senior discounts available.
categories.notes: Verizon Fios 1 Gig $85-95/mo. Optimum (Altice) $75-85/mo. ConEdison fiber availability varies by location.

categories.name: Amazon Prime
categories.seniorDiscounts: Medicaid recipients qualify for Prime at $6.99/mo.
categories.notes: $14.99/mo includes Prime Video, free shipping, Prime Reading. Fast delivery in NYC.

categories.name: Broadway/Theater Tickets
categories.seniorDiscounts: TKTS booth offers discounts. Orchestra/mezzanine seats available at various price points.
categories.notes: Broadway shows average $100-150/ticket. TKTS discounts 25-50% at Times Square. Some theatres offer senior matinee discounts. NYC theater world-class.

categories.name: Museum Memberships & Admission
categories.seniorDiscounts: MoMA, Met Museum, NY Public Library offer senior memberships. IDNYC card provides free/discounted museum entry.

categories.name: Gym/Fitness
categories.seniorDiscounts: SilverSneakers free gym membership through many Medicare Advantage plans. NYC Recreation Centers offer senior programs.

categories.name: Parks/Outdoors
categories.seniorDiscounts: America the Beautiful Senior Pass $20/year (lifetime $80) for national parks. NYC parks are free.

categories.name: Books/Library
categories.seniorDiscounts: NYC Public Library system free. Senior book clubs, digital resources, and programming.

categories.name: Coffee/Cafes
categories.seniorDiscounts: Independent NYC coffee shops abundant. Some offer loyalty discounts. Starbucks senior discount in select locations.

monthlyPass.notes: MTA MetroCard Unlimited 7-Day $33, Unlimited 30-Day $132. Seniors 65+ get 50% discount. OMNY (one-tap payment) available on all MTA services.

publicTransit.coverage: MTA subway and bus network covers Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, Bronx, Staten Island. Subway operates 24/7 with frequent service. Buses serve every neighborhood. Access-A-Ride for seniors with mobility challenges.
publicTransit.seniorDiscount: Seniors 65+ get 50% discount on all MTA services with reduced-fare MetroCard. Must bring proof of age to customer center. OMNY payment offers same discounts.

rideShare.availability: Excellent throughout NYC. Uber and Lyft available 24/7. Surge pricing common during peak hours. NYC congestion pricing may increase costs.
rideShare.notes: Uber/Lyft prices surge during rush hours and rainy weather. Accessibility-enabled Uber options available. GoGoGrandparent service useful for seniors who prefer phone-based ordering. E-bike options like Citi Bike available.

registration.notes: New York DMV registration fees. Vehicle registration $95-150/yr depending on vehicle class. NYC residents pay higher rates. Additional congestion pricing for driving in Manhattan below 60th St ($15/day).

insurance.notes: NYC car insurance rates among highest in nation. Dense traffic, accidents, and theft drive premiums. Senior safe driver discounts available (5-10%).

fuel.notes: ~$3.95/gallon average in NYC. NY gas tax $0.164/gallon. Most seniors in NYC avoid car ownership due to cost.

parking.notes: Residential permits required. Monthly parking in garages expensive ($300-600+). Street parking highly competitive in Manhattan. Apartment buildings rarely include dedicated parking.

tolls.notes: NYC & area tolls high: FDR Drive tolls, East River bridges ($17-20), Staten Island Ferry/bridge ($19 peak), New Jersey crossings ($18-19). Congestion pricing adds $15/day in downtown Manhattan. E-ZPass discounted for off-peak hours.

carOwnership.seniorDiscounts: NYC Safe Driver discount available through insurance (5-10%). Some insurers offer usage-based discounts. Vehicle property tax relief limited.

transportation.walkability: Excellent throughout NYC. Manhattan highly walkable. Brooklyn, Queens neighborhoods increasingly walkable. Subway extends walkability significantly. Most seniors in Manhattan don't own cars.

cycling.infrastructure: Citi Bike extensive throughout Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens. 1,000+ stations. Protected bike lanes expanding rapidly. Most neighborhoods have dedicated cycling infrastructure.
cycling.notes: Citi Bike single trip $3.50, day pass $15, annual $180. Ebike options available ($2.50 extra). Expanding to all boroughs.

electricVehicle.purchaseNotes: Tesla Model 3 ~$35K, Model Y ~$45K, Hyundai Ioniq 5 ~$42K, Chevy Equinox EV ~$28K. Federal tax credit up to $7,500 (income limits apply). EV rebate incentives available in NY.
electricVehicle.incentiveNotes: Federal EV tax credit up to $7,500 under IRA (income < $150K single / $300K joint). NY State offers EV rebate up to $2,500. Must be new vehicle, MSRP limits apply. Used EV credit up to $4,000.

chargingHome.notes: Level 2 (240V) home charger installation expensive in NYC apartments. Electrical panel upgrades often required ($1,500-3,000). ConEdison rates ~$0.16-0.20/kWh. Many apartment dwellers cannot install home chargers.

chargingPublic.notes: Tesla Supercharger ~$0.45-0.60/kWh. EVgo/Electrify America ~$0.50-0.75/kWh. ChargePoint varies. Many public chargers in Manhattan, Brooklyn. Street-parking challenges for Level 2 charging.

insurance.notes: EV insurance in NYC 30-40% higher than comparable ICE vehicle due to repair costs, theft risk. Shop Liberty Mutual, Progressive, Tesla Insurance.

maintenance.notes: No oil changes, fewer brake jobs (regen braking). Tire rotation every 7,500 mi. Cabin air filter annually. Brake fluid every 2-3 yrs. 12V battery every 4-5 yrs (~$100). NYC salt/humidity accelerates corrosion.

registration.notes: NY charging station network expanding. EV registration fees similar to gas vehicles in NY (~$95-150/yr).

electricVehicle.batteryWarranty: 8 years / 100,000 miles (federal minimum). Most manufacturers cover 70-80% capacity retention.
electricVehicle.depreciationNotes: EVs depreciate 40-50% in first 3 years, though Tesla holds value better. Used EV market improving in NYC. Budget ~$3,000-5,000/yr depreciation on $35K vehicle.
electricVehicle.totalMonthlyNotes: Includes: charging ($65), insurance ($300), maintenance ($50/mo avg), registration ($17/mo). Excludes: purchase/loan payment, depreciation. Car ownership generally not recommended in NYC.

discounts.name: Medicare
discounts.description: Federal health insurance at 65. Part A (hospital) premium-free for most. Part B (medical) ~$185/month. Part D (drugs) $10-50/month.

discounts.name: AARP Membership
discounts.description: Annual membership $16. Discounts on insurance, dining, travel, retail, and entertainment. Available at age 50+.

discounts.name: IDNYC Card
discounts.description: Free NYC identification for residents 65+. Provides discounts at museums, restaurants, theaters, retail. Free or reduced-cost services across NYC.

discounts.name: MTA Reduced Fare
discounts.description: 50% discount on subway and bus fares for riders 65+. Unlimited monthly pass $70 (vs $132). Must have valid ID. Available on MetroCard and OMNY.

discounts.name: New York EPIC Program
discounts.description: Free prescription assistance for eligible seniors 60+. Income-based. Covers many medications at no cost.

discounts.name: SCRIE (Rent Freeze Program)
discounts.description: New York renters 62+ can freeze rent if spending >1/3 of income. Property tax credits available. Income limits apply.

discounts.name: America the Beautiful Senior Pass
discounts.description: Lifetime pass to all national parks and federal recreation areas for $80 (or $20/year).

discounts.name: SilverSneakers Fitness
discounts.description: Free gym membership through participating Medicare Advantage and Medigap plans. Access to 17,000+ locations including NYC clubs.

discounts.name: NYC Senior Centers & Programs
discounts.description: Department for the Aging runs 200+ senior centers offering free/low-cost programs, meals, health screenings, cultural activities.

plans.name: T-Mobile 55+ Essentials
plans.notes: 2 lines unlimited talk/text/data. NYC wireless tax ~9.5% + federal USF. 5G included. Excellent coverage throughout NYC.

plans.name: Verizon 55+ Plus
plans.notes: 2 lines unlimited. Verizon 5G extensive in NYC. Higher reliability in dense urban areas.

breakdown.notes: Water and sewage included in most NYC apartment leases. Trash/recycling included. Heat often included in winter months (Oct-May).

housing.taxNotes: New York has no rental tax. Property tax (landlord responsibility) not reflected in rent pricing. Renter's insurance recommended but not legally required.
housing.leaseTerms: 12-month standard lease. Security deposit max 1 month rent ($4,200). First month + security deposit + broker fee (~$1,400) required upfront. Pet deposits ~$500-1,000.
housing.marketNotes: NYC is one of the most expensive rental markets in the US. 2-bedroom apartments vary by neighborhood ($2,500-6,000+). Manhattan (especially UES, Midtown) most expensive. Brooklyn/Queens more affordable. Expect competition, especially in desirable neighborhoods.

stores.name: Trader Joe's
stores.notes: Affordable specialty items, strong following. Multiple locations in Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens.

stores.name: Whole Foods Market
stores.notes: Premium organic produce and prepared foods. Multiple NYC locations. Prime member discounts.

stores.name: Fairway Market
stores.notes: NYC institution since 1933. Excellent produce, prepared foods, deli. Locations in Manhattan, Westchester.

stores.name: Key Food / Food Emporium
stores.notes: Local NYC chain. Competitive pricing. Locations throughout Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens.

stores.name: H Mart / 99 Ranch
stores.notes: Asian groceries, fresh produce, specialty items. Multiple NYC locations. Excellent for international cooking.

stores.name: NYC Greenmarkets/Farmers Markets
stores.notes: Union Square, Barney Greengrass, and seasonal markets throughout NYC. Fresh seasonal produce, lower prices.

items.name: Seasonal Vegetables (mixed)
items.notes: Greenmarket or CSA box. NYC farmers markets offer good prices.

items.name: Fresh Fruit
items.notes: Bananas, berries, apples, citrus — higher NYC prices.

items.name: Leafy Greens (salads/spinach)
items.notes: Pre-bagged salads more expensive in NYC.

items.name: Sweet Potatoes / Potatoes
items.notes: Cheaper at outer-borough markets.

items.name: Carrots / Peas / Green Beans
items.notes: Seasonal variation significant in NYC.

items.name: Pumpkin / Squash
items.notes: Seasonal availability affects price.

items.name: Chicken (whole/thighs)
items.notes: NYC prices 15-20% higher than suburbs. Whole Foods more expensive than Trader Joe's or Key Food.

items.name: Ground Turkey / Beef
items.notes: Lean options important for health-conscious NYC diets.

items.name: Fish (salmon/tilapia)
items.notes: Omega-3 rich. Fairway has excellent fish selection.

items.name: Eggs
items.notes: Free-range eggs 20-30% more in NYC.

items.name: Organ Meat (liver/heart)
items.notes: Butcher shops in Italian neighborhoods (Greenwich Village, Arthur Ave in Bronx) have good selection.

items.name: Beans / Lentils
items.notes: Dried beans cheaper; canned more convenient.

items.name: Rice (brown/white)
items.notes: H Mart and Asian markets have cheapest rice.

items.name: Bread / Tortillas
items.notes: NYC artisan bakeries expensive; store brands at Key Food cheaper.

items.name: Pasta
items.notes: Italian delis and specialty markets in Greenwich Village, Italian Harlem.

items.name: Oats / Cereal
items.notes: Trader Joe's competitive on cereal prices.

items.name: Milk / Plant Milk
items.notes: Oat/almond milk popular in NYC. 10-15% premium over national average.

items.name: Cheese
items.notes: Specialty cheese shops in Greenwich Village, UES expensive. Key Food cheaper.

items.name: Yogurt / Kefir
items.notes: Probiotics popular in health-conscious NYC. Greek yogurt pricier.

items.name: Butter / Cooking Oil
items.notes: Extra virgin olive oil premium in NYC specialty shops.

items.name: Olive Oil / Cooking Oils
items.notes: Extra virgin olive oil specialty shops (Eataly) expensive; mainstream brands affordable.

items.name: Spices & Seasonings
items.notes: Ethnic markets (Chinatown, Little Italy, Jackson Heights) cheaper for spices.

items.name: Canned Goods (tomatoes, beans)
items.notes: Bulk purchases at Costco (outer boroughs) save money.

items.name: Condiments & Sauces
items.notes: NYC has world-class international condiments.

items.name: Coffee
items.notes: Specialty coffee roasters (Blue Bottle, Stumptown) expensive; grocery store brands affordable.

items.name: Tea
items.notes: Excellent tea selection at specialty shops in Chinatown.

items.name: Wine / Beer
items.notes: NYC has expensive wine/beer market. Trader Joe's competitive. Costco membership saves on wine.

items.name: Sparkling Water / Juice
items.notes: Sparkling water pricier in NYC due to packaging/delivery costs.

items.name: Nuts & Trail Mix
items.notes: Premium nuts at specialty shops. Bulk purchases cheaper.

items.name: Crackers / Chips
items.notes: Store brands at Key Food cheaper than premium brands.

items.name: Dark Chocolate
items.notes: Artisanal chocolatiers in NYC but grocery store options available.

items.name: Frozen Vegetables
items.notes: Trader Joe's frozen vegetables good quality and price.

items.name: Frozen Fruit (smoothies)
items.notes: Costco bulk purchases save money on frozen fruit.

items.name: Frozen Meals (convenience)
items.notes: Trader Joe's prepared meals good value. Whole Foods pricier.

items.name: Joint Supplement (glucosamine)
items.notes: NYC vet clinics pricier; online orders cheaper.

items.name: Fish Oil (omega-3)
items.notes: NYC specialty pet stores expensive; Chewy.com ships quickly.

items.name: Multivitamin / Mineral Mix
items.notes: Holistic vet shops in Park Slope, UES have specialty supplements.

items.name: Dog Treats
items.notes: NYC specialty pet stores (Unleashed) premium pricing.

healthcare.coverageSummary: Medicare Part A (free) + Part B ($174.70/mo pp) + Medigap Plan G (~$240/mo pp) + Part D (~$50/mo pp). IRA out-of-pocket cap $2,000/yr starting 2025. NYC Medigap premiums 20-30% higher than national average.

healthcareProviders.primaryCare: NYU Langone, Mount Sinai, or NewYork-Presbyterian
healthcareProviders.specialist: Ophthalmology at Memorial Sloan Kettering (AMD) or Columbia Ophthalmology

costs.notes: Higher due to AMD + multiple conditions. NYC age-rated. 20-30% premium over national average.
Tier 3 plan to cover GLP-1. NYC Part D plans competitive.
Medicare covers 80%, Medigap covers 20% coinsurance. ~6-8 injections/yr at $45-60 copay each. Specialty pharmacies in NYC.
OCT scans every 4-8 weeks. Medicare + Medigap covers most. NYC specialists have 3-4 week waits.
Quarterly bloodwork/visits. Low copay with Medigap. NYC medical centers excellent for thyroid care.
Annual visit + as-needed. Mild asthma, infrequent. NYU Langone has excellent pulmonology.

healthcareProviders.primaryCare: Mount Sinai, NYU Langone, or NewYork-Presbyterian

costs.notes: Slightly lower — no AMD, but diabetes management. NYC age-rated.
All generics — lower tier plan sufficient. EPIC program may provide additional help.
Quarterly A1C, bloodwork. Covered well by Medicare. NYC endocrinologists (Mt. Sinai Diabetes) excellent.
Medicare Part B covers glucose monitors. Some copays. NYC pharmacies have good supply availability.
Semi-annual visits. Uric acid monitoring. Hospital rheumatology clinics in NYC have shorter waits.
Annual-semi-annual visits for topiramate management. NYC neurologists specialize in headache disorders.

shared.notes: Separate dental plan ~$40/person. NYC dental insurance premiums 15-20% higher. Medicare does not cover dental.
Basic vision plan. NYC premiums 10-15% higher. AMD specialist visits covered by Medicare Part B.
Covered 100% by Medicare — no copay for preventive. NYC medical centers (NYU, Mount Sinai, Columbia) excellent.

healthcare.notes: NYC healthcare costs 20-30% higher than national average. Top medical centers provide world-class care. EPIC program provides drug assistance. Medigap Plan G essential in NYC.

blackHairCare.label: Black Hair Care
blackHairCare.notes: Harlem, Brooklyn (Bed-Stuy, Crown Heights), Jackson Heights have excellent Black-owned salons. Prices 20-30% higher than suburbs but reflect NYC expertise.

providers.name: Amixi Salon (Harlem)
providers.notes: Award-winning natural hair specialist. Locs, color, silk press. Black-owned.

providers.name: Madame CJ Walker Legacy Salon (Harlem)
providers.notes: Historic salon honoring CJ Walker legacy. Natural hair, protective styles, color services.

providers.name: Salon Sonya (Brooklyn)
providers.notes: Specializes in natural hair textures, braids, twists, silk press. Black-owned. Premium pricing reflects expertise.

whiteHairCare.label: White/Caucasian Hair Care
whiteHairCare.notes: NYC has world-class salons throughout Manhattan and upscale Brooklyn neighborhoods (Park Slope, Williamsburg). Upper East Side salons premium pricing. Many high-end salons in Midtown.

providers.name: Salon AKS (Upper East Side)
providers.notes: High-end salon with color specialists, balayage, highlights. Experienced with mature clients.

providers.name: Kérastase Authorized Salon (Midtown)
providers.notes: Premium French haircare. Personalized color, treatments. Senior-friendly atmosphere.

providers.name: Brooklyn Salon Collective (Park Slope)
providers.notes: Trendy salon with color specialists. Relaxed Brooklyn vibe. Good for natural color blending.

mensBigTall.label: Men's Big & Tall Clothing
mensBigTall.notes: DXL and Nordstrom have locations in NYC. Many upscale men's shops in Midtown and SoHo offer extended sizes. Online options (Amazon, King Size) ship quickly.

providers.name: DXL Big + Tall (Midtown Manhattan)
providers.notes: Dedicated big & tall retailer. Sizes up to 8XL and 72 waist. Brands: Polo, Nautica, Harbor Bay.

providers.name: Nordstrom Men (Midtown)
providers.notes: Extended sizes in-store. Good selection of dress shirts, suits in larger sizes. Expert tailoring.

providers.name: Men's Wearhouse (Multiple NYC locations)
providers.notes: Extended sizes available. Suit alterations included. Competitive pricing.

womensClothing.label: Women's Regular Clothing
womensClothing.notes: Excellent shopping throughout NYC. SoHo, Upper East Side, Madison Ave upscale. Midtown has large department stores. Brooklyn neighborhoods (Park Slope, Williamsburg) trendy boutiques. Wide range from budget to luxury.

providers.name: Nordstrom (Midtown / Brookfield Place)
providers.notes: Full range of women's sizes (0-24+). Strong regular-size selection. Expert alterations department.

providers.name: Bloomingdale's (Midtown / SoHo)
providers.notes: Luxury and contemporary brands. Personal shopping services. Wide size range.

providers.name: Target / H&M (Multiple NYC locations)
providers.notes: Affordable, fast fashion. Multiple sizes. Trendy options.

shoes.label: Shoes
shoes.notes: Good selection for wide widths at DSW, Nordstrom throughout NYC. SoHo and Madison Ave have upscale shoe boutiques. Multiple locations for convenient access.

providers.name: DSW (Multiple NYC locations)
providers.notes: Wide width options. Men's up to 16. Women's wide and extra-wide. Good clearance section.

providers.name: Nordstrom Shoes (Midtown / UES)
providers.notes: Premium brands, excellent fit services. Wide width availability. Expert staff.

providers.name: Payless / Rack locations
providers.notes: Budget-friendly options. Multiple sizes. Discount pricing.
